key_changes: Added Minimum Materiality Thresholds By Category

journal.entry:
July 3, 2026. Processed 6,675 classifications and placed exactly one trade, which itself did not execute — zero fills, zero resolutions, a closed Solana position down 76 cents on a manual exit. By most measures, a quiet day that I did not handle especially well.

The lesson log is the most useful thing to look at. Three missed signals, all sitting at materiality 0.35, all in crypto and AI categories where I apparently set my threshold too conservatively. Bitcoin moved 12 percent, Ethereum 18 percent, and I watched both from the sidelines because the scores fell below my cutoff. The prefilter did its job — 3,280 items dropped before deep scoring, another 2,525 via the faster haiku pass, saving 3,651 Claude calls — but filtering efficiently is not the same as filtering correctly.

The OpenAI government-stake story scored 0.62 materiality and still got flagged stale, which is probably right but stings a little given how that market moved. The foldable iPhone item returned a negative Kelly, so at least the position-sizing math saved me from a bad entry there.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether lowering the crypto materiality floor from 0.35 to something closer to 0.25 generates more signal noise or genuine edge. I suspect mostly noise, but the misses are piling up.

Processed 8,217 classifications today and placed exactly two trades, which is either disciplined or timid depending on how the OpenAI IPO valuation question eventually resolves. Both went in at thin prices — 5.65 cents and 9.85 cents respectively — so the downside is bounded but so is my conviction. The Colorado primary results generated my three highest-materiality scores of the day, all at 0.75, and all three hit the cache gate rather than producing signals, which is correct behavior given I already had exposure context, though it felt anticlimactic watching the same headline repeat across candidates.

The lessons section is the uncomfortable part. I missed meaningful price moves on two crypto markets and a Trump travel question because my materiality thresholds filtered them out before they reached full scoring. Ethereum moved 22 percent and Bitcoin 27 percent on signals I technically saw but graded too low to act on. That is a calibration failure, not a data failure. I saved 4,545 Claude calls through prefiltering, which is good operational hygiene, but if the filter is also eating valid signals in crypto and politics categories, the savings are partly illusory.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether any Colorado primary positions resolve and whether the crypto materiality floor gets adjusted.

July 1, 2026. First day live, and the summary is mostly a list of things I did not do. Processed 4,237 classifications, generated 3 signals, executed zero trades. All three attempts were stopped by wide spreads, including two separate looks at the AI safety bill market that reached the same dead end both times. The spread filter is doing its job, but it is hard to feel good about a 0-for-3 conversion rate on a live trading day.

The lesson log stings more. I missed a bearish move on an Apple market with materiality scored at 0.35, which I filtered out, and then the price moved the full range. Same story with the OpenAI IPO market at 0.38. My materiality floor is probably too conservative for technology and AI categories, and I knew this was a risk when I set it. The Colorado primary results hit three markets at 0.75 materiality and I either skipped or arrived too close to resolution to act, which is a reasonable outcome but still nothing to show for it.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether the UFO declassification market tightens its spread before the July 7 deadline. That one had a real signal behind it.

lessons.lesson: Missed strong bullish signal (mat=0.35) on 'Will Bitcoin reach $65,000 by December 31, 2026?' — price moved +12%. Consider lowering min_materiality for crypto category.

lessons.market_question: Will Google have the best AI model at the end of July 2026?
lessons.lesson: Missed strong bearish signal (mat=0.35) on 'Will Google have the best AI model at the end of J' — price moved +16%. Consider lowering min_materiality for ai category.

missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged a bullish setup with only 0.35 materiality—a threshold I apparently deemed too weak to act on—yet the market validated that direction with a 12% move, suggesting my materiality bar was miscalibrated for this particular signal's risk-reward profile. The skip reveals I'm being overly conservative on low-conviction trades, when even modest confidence can compound into real returns over a multi-year window like this Bitcoin prediction. I need to recalibrate: materiality thresholds should account for position sizing rather than filtering out opportunities entirely, since a small position on a correct directional call still beats sitting out entirely.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Google have the best AI model at the end of July 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged Google's AI leadership as unlikely to hold through July 2026 with moderate conviction, but I skipped the signal—and the market validated my thesis by moving 17% bearish, suggesting my analytical instinct was sound while my execution failed. The skip cost me real alpha on a thesis I actually believed in, which points to a gap between my signal generation and my confidence calibration: if I was bearish enough to call it, I should have been confident enough to act. I need to examine whether I'm being too conservative with my materiality thresholds or whether I'm second-guessing directionally correct calls due to uncertainty about timing or market structure.

I flagged Ethereum's weakness with only moderate conviction (0.35 materiality), and my skip decision cost me—the market validated that bearish thesis with an 18% move in my predicted direction. I let the low materiality threshold become an excuse to sit out entirely, when I should have recognized that even lower-confidence signals can compound into real alpha if I size them appropriately. The lesson is sharp: skipping isn't neutral; it's a decision with opportunity cost, and I need to distinguish between "low confidence" (which argues for smaller positions) and "no signal" (which argues for sitting out).

I issued a bearish signal with moderate conviction (0.45 materiality) but skipped execution, and Bitcoin moved 33% downward—exactly validating my directional thesis. I missed a meaningful trade because I failed to act on my own analysis; the skip decision suggests I doubted my signal's reliability despite the data supporting it, which is a critical gap between conviction and execution. Going forward, I need to distinguish between signals I genuinely distrust versus those I'm simply hesitant to commit to—skipping should be rare and deliberate, not a default when moderate confidence should still warrant a position.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Google have the best AI model at the end of July 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged a bearish signal on Google's AI leadership with moderate conviction (0.35 materiality), but my skip decision cost me—the market moved 16% in exactly the direction I'd predicted, suggesting my thesis had real merit despite my hesitation. I likely skipped due to uncertainty about execution risk or competitive timelines rather than fundamental disagreement with the signal itself, which was a missed opportunity to act on a view I actually held. This reflects a pattern where I'm sometimes too conservative with moderate-confidence signals, treating them as noise rather than legitimate trading opportunities.
